draft.introduction: The Ars Poetica (c. 19 BCE) is Horace’s verse epistle on the craft of poetry. The phrase ut pictura poesis (“as is painting, so is poetry”) became the most influential dictum in Western aesthetic theory, governing debates about the relationship between visual and literary arts.

translation.text: As is painting, so is poetry: some works will captivate you more if you stand nearer, others if you stand further off. One loves the dark; another, which does not fear the critic’s sharp eye, wishes to be seen in the light. One pleases once; another will still please if looked at ten times over. The aim of the poet is either to benefit or to delight, or to say things at once pleasing and useful for life.
translation.original_text: Ut pictura poesis: erit quae, si propius stes, te capiat magis, et quaedam, si longius abstes. Aut prodesse volunt aut delectare poetae aut simul et iucunda et idonea dicere vitae.

translator_notes.note: Ut pictura poesis was taken out of context for centuries to mean that poetry should imitate painting and vice versa. Horace’s actual point is more modest: different poems, like different paintings, work best under different conditions of viewing.
